items.title: Downloads
items.text:
The desktop app is the full r0astr interface wrapped in Electron. All Strudel packages ship bundled—core, mini notation, transpiler, Web Audio, tonal, soundfonts, draw, xen, osc, serial, csound, and CodeMirror—so startup is instant with no network fetch required. Samples and SoundFonts stream on first play from the Strudel CDN.

The desktop build also starts an Express + WebSocket server on localhost:3000, enabling the remote control surface (open /remote.html on a tablet or phone on the same network) and a REST / WebSocket API for programmatic control from external tools, scripts, or MCP agents.

items.title: Installation Instructions
items.text: macOSWindowsLinux

items.title: Install
items.text:
1. Download the .dmg file for your Mac (Intel or Apple Silicon)
  2. Open the downloaded .dmg file
  3. Drag r0astr to your Applications folder
  4. Eject the disk image

items.title: First Launch
items.text:
Security Warning

macOS may show a warning because r0astr is not notarized by Apple.

If you see "r0astr is damaged" or "unidentified developer":

  1. Right-click (or Control-click) the app
  2. Select Open from the menu
  3. Click Open in the dialog

Or use Terminal:

xattr -cr /Applications/r0astr.app

Then open the app normally.

items.title: Install (Installer Version)
items.text:
1. Download r0astr-Setup-x.x.x.exe
  2. Run the downloaded .exe file
  3. Follow the installation wizard
  4. Launch from Start Menu or Desktop shortcut

items.title: Portable Version
items.text:
1. Download r0astr-x.x.x.exe (portable)
  2. Place the .exe anywhere you like
  3. Double-click to run (no installation needed)

SmartScreen Warning

Windows may show a SmartScreen warning because r0astr is not signed.

To bypass:

  1. Click More info
  2. Click Run anyway

items.title: AppImage (Recommended)
items.text:
Works on most distributions.

# Make it executable
chmod +x r0astr-*.AppImage

# Run it
./r0astr-*.AppImage

Desktop Integration

Use AppImageLauncher to integrate with your desktop environment.

items.title: Debian/Ubuntu (.deb)
items.text:
# Install
sudo dpkg -i r0astr_*.deb

# Fix any dependency issues
sudo apt-get install -f

Launch from your application menu or run r0astr in terminal.

items.title: Verifying Installation
items.text:
After installation, launch r0astr. You should see:

  1. Main interface with panels and a master panel
  2. Default patterns loaded in each panel

items.title: First Steps
items.text:
1. Click anywhere to enable audio
  2. Click Play on Panel 1 to hear the default pattern
  3. Try the Getting Started guide

items.title: Audio Check
items.text:
If you don't hear sound:

  • Check your system volume
  • Ensure audio output is configured
  • Try clicking on the page first (browsers require user interaction)
  • See Troubleshooting

items.title: Pattern Examples
items.text: A collection of ready-to-use patterns for r0astr. Copy these into your cards and experiment!

items.title: Basic Four-on-the-Floor
items.text:
s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd, hh*8").gain(0.8)
Classic house/techno beat with kick, snare, and hi-hats.

items.title: Breakbeat
items.text:
s("bd ~ bd ~, ~ sd ~ [sd sd], [~ hh]*4").fast(1.5)
A more complex breakbeat pattern with syncopation.

items.title: Minimal Techno
items.text:
s("bd ~ ~ ~, ~ ~ sd ~, ~ hh ~ hh").delay(0.125).delayfeedback(0.4)
Sparse techno pattern with delay for movement.

items.title: Deep Sub Bass
items.text:
note("c2 ~ c2 ~").s("sawtooth").lpf(slider(400, 100, 1000)).gain(0.6)
Simple but effective sub bass with controllable filter.

items.title: Funky Bass
items.text:
note("c2 ~ eb2 f2").s("sawtooth").lpf(600).decay(0.1).fast(2)
More rhythmic bass line with movement.

items.title: Acid Bass
items.text:
note("c2 ~ eb2 ~").s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(slider(800, 200, 4000))
  .resonance(10)
  .gain(0.5)
Classic acid sound with resonant filter sweep.

items.title: Simple Arpeggio
items.text:
n("0 2 3 5").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").lpf(600).fast(2)
Basic melodic arpeggio in C minor.

items.title: Chord Progression
items.text:
note("<c3'maj7 f3'maj7 g3'7 a3'm7>")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(1000)
  .room(0.5)
  .slow(4)
Jazz-inspired chord progression with reverb.

items.title: Euclidean Melody
items.text:
note("c4 d4 e4 g4 a4").euclidean(3, 8).scale("C:minor").s("sine")
Euclidean rhythm applied to melodic notes.

items.title: Pad
items.text:
note("c3 e3 g3")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(400)
  .room(0.9)
  .gain(slider(0.3, 0, 0.6))
  .slow(8)
Slow-moving ambient pad with heavy reverb.

items.title: Drone
items.text:
note("c2 g2")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(300)
  .room(0.95)
  .delay(0.5)
  .delayfeedback(0.7)
  .gain(0.4)
Deep drone with delay and reverb.

items.title: Texture
items.text:
s("~ ~ click ~")
  .speed(rand.range(0.5, 2))
  .room(1)
  .delay(slider(0.3, 0, 0.8))
  .gain(0.3)
Random textural clicks with effects.

items.title: Minimal Set (4 Cards)
items.text:
Card 1 - Kick: 

s("bd*4").gain(0.9)

Card 2 - Hi-hats: 

s("~ hh ~ hh").delay(0.125).gain(0.6)

Card 3 - Bass: 

note("c2 ~ eb2 ~").s("sawtooth").lpf(slider(500, 200, 2000)).gain(0.6)

Card 4 - Lead: 

n("0 3 5 7").scale("C4:minor")
  .s("triangle")
  .lpf(800)
  .room(0.4)
  .fast(2)
  .gain(slider(0.4, 0, 0.7))

items.title: Ambient Set (4 Cards)
items.text:
Card 1 - Low Drone: 

note("c2").s("sawtooth").lpf(200).room(0.9).gain(0.3).slow(8)

Card 2 - Mid Drone: 

note("g2 c3").s("sawtooth").lpf(400).room(0.9).gain(0.25).slow(6)

Card 3 - Texture: 

s("~ ~ click ~")
  .speed(rand.range(0.3, 1.5))
  .room(1)
  .delay(0.5)
  .gain(0.2)
  .slow(2)

Card 4 - Melody: 

n("0 2 4 7").scale("C5:minor")
  .s("sine")
  .room(0.7)
  .gain(slider(0.3, 0, 0.5))
  .slow(4)

items.title: Using Randomness
items.text:
note("c3 d3 e3 g3").sometimes(x => x.fast(2))
Randomly speeds up the pattern sometimes.

items.title: Polyrhythms
items.text:
s("bd*3, hh*4, sd*5").slow(2)
Three different rhythmic patterns creating complex timing.

items.title: Degrading
items.text:
s("hh*8").degrade(0.3)
Randomly removes 30% of events for variation.

items.title: Chopping Patterns
items.text:
s("bd hh sd hh").chunk(4, (x, i) => i % 2 ? x.fast(2) : x)
Processes pattern chunks differently.

items.title: Interactive Control Tips
items.text:
Use sliders for live performance control:

// Wet/dry mix
s("bd*4").room(slider(0.3, 0, 1))

// Tempo variation
s("hh*8").fast(slider(1, 0.5, 2))

// Filter cutoff
note("c2").lpf(slider(800, 100, 5000))

// Gain/volume
s("bd ~ sd ~").gain(slider(0.7, 0, 1))

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Try combining these patterns in different ways
  • Modify parameters to make them your own
  • Check the Strudel documentation for more functions
  • Experiment with Remote Control for live performance

items.title: Getting Started
items.text: Get up and running with r0astr in under 5 minutes.

items.title: Choose Your Path
items.text:
Try in Browser (Instant)Run Locally (Development)Desktop App

The fastest way to start. No installation needed.

  1. Open the app: Launch r0astr
  2. Click anywhere on the page to enable audio (browser requirement)
  3. You're ready! Continue to Your First Pattern below

For development or offline use.

Prerequisites: Node.js 18+ and npm

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/piatra-automation/r0astr.git
cd r0astr

# Install dependencies
npm install

# Start development server
npm run dev

Open http://localhost:5173 in your browser.

For the full experience with remote control.

Download for your platform

items.title: Your First Pattern
items.text: Let's make some noise.

items.title: Step 1: Find Panel 1
items.text: You'll see several panels on screen. Each panel is an independent instrument. Find Panel 1.

items.title: Step 2: Enter a Pattern
items.text:
Click in the editor and paste this drum pattern:

s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd, hh*8").gain(0.8)

What does this mean?

  • s("...") plays samples (sounds)
  • bd*4 = bass drum 4 times
  • ~ sd ~ sd = snare on beats 2 and 4 (~ is silence)
  • hh*8 = hi-hat 8 times
  • .gain(0.8) = volume at 80%

items.title: Step 3: Hit Play
items.text:
Click the Play button on Panel 1. You should hear a basic drum beat!

Congratulations!

You just wrote your first live coding pattern.

items.title: Add a Second Instrument
items.text: Now let's layer in some bass.

items.title: Step 1: Find Panel 2
items.text: Leave Panel 1 playing. Find Panel 2.

items.title: Step 2: Enter a Bass Pattern
items.text:
Paste this into Panel 2:

note("c2 ~ c2 e2").s("sawtooth").lpf(400).gain(0.6)

What does this mean?

  • note("...") plays synth notes
  • c2, e2 = notes (C and E, octave 2)
  • .s("sawtooth") = sawtooth wave synth
  • .lpf(400) = low-pass filter at 400Hz
  • .gain(0.6) = volume at 60%

items.title: Step 3: Hit Play on Panel 2
items.text: Click Play. The bass joins the drums — perfectly in sync!

items.title: Try More Patterns
items.text: Add these to Panels 3 and 4:

items.title: Panel 3: Melody
items.text: n("0 2 3 5 3 2").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").gain(0.5)

items.title: Panel 4: Ambient Pad
items.text:
n("0 3 7").scale("C3:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(600).room(0.8).slow(4).gain(0.4)

Now you have a full arrangement with drums, bass, melody, and ambient texture!

items.title: What You've Learned
items.text:
• Panels are independent instruments
  • s() plays samples, note() plays synths
  • All panels share the same clock (they stay in sync)
  • Play/Pause controls each panel independently

items.title: Remote Control
items.text: Control r0astr from your tablet, phone, or any device on your network.

items.title: Overview
items.text:
r0astr includes a remote control interface that lets you control panels from a secondary device. Perfect for:

  • Using an iPad as a control surface while coding on your laptop
  • Triggering panels from a phone during live performance
  • Controlling r0astr from across the room

items.title: 1. Start r0astr
items.text:
Run r0astr on your main computer:

npm run dev

Or launch the desktop app.

items.title: 2. Find Your IP Address
items.text:
macOSWindowsLinux

Open Terminal and run:

ipconfig getifaddr en0

Or go to System Settings → Network → Your connection → Details → IP Address

Open Command Prompt and run:

ipconfig

Look for "IPv4 Address" under your active network adapter.

Open Terminal and run:

hostname -I | awk '{print $1}'

items.title: 3. Connect from Your Device
items.text:
On your tablet or phone:

  1. Make sure you're on the same WiFi network
  2. Open your browser
  3. Go to: http://YOUR-IP:5173/remote.html

For example: http://192.168.1.100:5173/remote.html

items.title: Network Requirements
items.text:
Same Network Required

The remote device MUST be on the same local network as the computer running r0astr.

items.title: Requirements
items.text: Requirement Details Same WiFi Both devices on the same network Port 5173 Must not be blocked by firewall No VPN VPN can interfere with local connections

items.title: Guest Networks
items.text: Most "guest" WiFi networks isolate devices from each other. If you can't connect, try the main network instead.

items.title: Remote Interface Features
items.text: The remote interface provides:

items.title: Panel Controls
items.text:
• Play/Pause buttons for each panel
  • Visual indicators showing which panels are playing
  • Panel numbering matches the main interface

items.title: Global Controls
items.text:
• Stop All - Immediately stops all panels
  • Connection status - Shows if connected to r0astr

items.title: Auto-Reconnection
items.text:
If the connection drops:

  1. The interface shows "Disconnected"
  2. Automatically attempts to reconnect
  3. Reconnects when r0astr becomes available again

items.title: "Disconnected" Status
items.text:
Cause: Cannot reach the r0astr server.

Solutions:

  1. Verify r0astr is running on your computer
  2. Check you're using the correct IP address
  3. Ensure both devices are on the same WiFi
  4. Try refreshing the remote page

items.title: Can't Connect Initially
items.text:
Common issues:

Problem Solution Wrong IP Re-check your computer's IP address Different networks Connect both devices to same WiFi Guest network Use main network instead Firewall blocking Allow port 5173 in firewall settings VPN active Disable VPN on both devices

items.title: Intermittent Disconnects
items.text:
Possible causes:

  • Weak WiFi signal
  • Network congestion
  • Sleep/standby modes

Solutions:

  • Move closer to WiFi router
  • Keep devices awake during performance
  • Consider a dedicated WiFi for performances

items.title: Finding Your IP - Common Mistakes
items.text: Mistake Correct Approach Using localhost Use your actual network IP (192.168.x.x) Using 127.0.0.1 This only works on the same machine Old IP after network change Re-check IP address after WiFi changes

items.title: Security
items.text:
Local Network Only

The remote control is designed for local network use only. No authentication is enforced — anyone on your network can connect. Use on trusted networks only.

items.title: Tips for Live Performance
items.text:
• Test the connection at the venue beforehand
  • Bookmark the remote URL on your device
  • Disable auto-sleep on your remote device
  • Use Stop All for clean transitions
  • Have a backup plan if WiFi fails

items.title: WebSocket Protocol
items.text: The remote control uses WebSocket for real-time communication. See the API Reference for the full protocol specification.

items.title: Related
items.text:
• API Reference — REST and WebSocket endpoints
  • Troubleshooting
  • FAQ

items.title: Usage Guide
items.text: Learn how to use r0astr effectively for live coding and music composition.

items.title: Interface Overview
items.text:
The r0astr interface consists of:

  • 4 Instrument Cards - Each card is an independent instrument
  • Master Panel - Global controls (if configured)
  • Code Editors - Write your pattern code in each card
  • Play/Pause Buttons - Control each instrument individually

items.title: Starting a Pattern
items.text:
1. Write or edit pattern code in a card's textarea
  2. Click the Play button for that card
  3. The pattern starts playing immediately

items.title: Updating a Pattern
items.text:
1. Edit the code while the pattern is playing
  2. Click Play again to apply changes
  3. The update happens on the next cycle boundary

items.title: Pausing a Pattern
items.text:
• Click Pause to mute that specific instrument
  • Other instruments continue playing
  • The pattern stays synchronized but produces no sound

items.title: Stopping a Pattern
items.text:
• Click Stop (if available) to completely stop and reset the pattern
  • Or use Pause to mute without stopping

items.title: Sounds and Samples
items.text:
// Play a sample
s("bd")

// Play multiple samples in sequence
s("bd hh sd hh")

// Layer patterns
s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd")

items.title: Notes and Melodies
items.text:
// Specific notes
note("c3 e3 g3")

// Scale degrees
n("0 2 4").scale("C:minor")

// Chords
note("<c3'maj7 f3'maj7>")

items.title: Timing and Rhythm
items.text:
// Multiply pattern (4 kicks per cycle)
s("bd*4")

// Subdivide (hi-hats twice as fast)
s("bd [hh hh]")

// Speed up pattern
s("bd hh sd hh").fast(2)

// Slow down pattern
s("bd hh sd hh").slow(2)

items.title: Sliders for Interactive Control
items.text:
Use slider() to create interactive controls:

// Basic slider: slider(default, min, max)
note("c2").lpf(slider(800, 100, 5000))

This creates a slider widget in the UI that you can adjust in real-time!

items.title: Slider Examples
items.text:
// Volume control
s("bd*4").gain(slider(0.8, 0, 1))

// Filter sweep
note("c2").lpf(slider(800, 100, 5000))

// Room size (reverb)
s("bd hh sd hh").room(slider(0.5, 0, 1))

// Delay time
s("hh*8").delay(slider(0.25, 0, 0.5))

items.title: Filters
items.text:
// Low-pass filter
.lpf(800)

// High-pass filter
.hpf(200)

// Band-pass filter
.bpf(1000)

items.title: Time-Based Effects
items.text:
// Reverb (room size)
.room(0.5)

// Delay
.delay(0.25)

// Delay with feedback
.delay(0.25).delayfeedback(0.6)

items.title: Dynamics
items.text:
// Volume
.gain(0.8)

// Velocity (for note-based patterns)
.velocity(0.7)

items.title: Synchronization
items.text:
All patterns share a common clock:

  • Patterns stay in sync automatically
  • You can start/stop cards independently
  • Timing relationships are maintained

items.title: Tips for Live Performance
items.text:
1. Prepare patterns - Write variations before performing
  2. Use sliders - Control parameters in real-time
  3. Layer gradually - Start with drums, add bass, then melody
  4. Mute strategically - Use Pause to create breaks and builds
  5. Remote control - Use an iPad for hands-on control (see Remote Control)

items.title: Build and Release
items.text:
// Start simple
s("bd*4")

// Add complexity
s("bd*4, hh*8")

// Add variation
s("bd*4, hh*8, ~ sd ~ sd")

items.title: Call and Response
items.text:
Use two cards for melodic conversation:

Card 1: 

note("c4 d4 e4").slow(2)

Card 2: 

note("g3 a3 b3").slow(2).late(1)

items.title: Drone and Melody
items.text:
Card 1 (Drone): 

note("c2").s("sawtooth").lpf(300).room(0.9).slow(4)

Card 2 (Melody): 

n("0 2 3 5 7").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").fast(2)

items.title: Frequently Asked Questions
items.text: Quick answers to common questions.

items.title: What is r0astr?
items.text: r0astr is a multi-instrument live coding interface built on Strudel. It provides a panel-based UI where each panel is an independent instrument, all synchronized to a shared clock.

items.title: Is r0astr free?
items.text: Yes, r0astr is open source and free to use under the AGPL-3.0 license.

items.title: What browsers are supported?
items.text:
• Chrome (recommended)
  • Firefox
  • Safari (macOS 14+)
  • Edge

items.title: Do I need to install anything?
items.text: No! You can try r0astr in your browser immediately. For offline use and remote control features, download the desktop app.

items.title: Why is there no sound?
items.text: Browsers require user interaction before playing audio. Click anywhere on the page, then try playing your pattern. See Troubleshooting for more help.

items.title: How do I stop all sounds?
items.text: Click the Stop All button in the master panel, or use keyboard shortcut (coming soon).

items.title: Can I use my own samples?
items.text: Yes! See the Strudel samples documentation for instructions.

items.title: What's the difference between s() and note()?
items.text:
• s("bd") plays samples (pre-recorded sounds)
  • note("c3") plays synthesizers (generated tones)

items.title: How do sliders work?
items.text:
Use slider(default, min, max) in your pattern:

note("c2").lpf(slider(800, 100, 5000))

This creates a control that you can adjust in real-time.

items.title: How do I set up remote control?
items.text:
1. Start r0astr on your computer
  2. Find your computer's local IP (e.g., 192.168.1.100)
  3. On your iPad/phone, open http://192.168.1.100:5173/remote.html
  4. Both devices must be on the same WiFi network

See Remote Control for detailed instructions.

items.title: Why won't my remote connect?
items.text:
• Ensure both devices are on the same WiFi
  • Check that r0astr is running on your computer
  • Try refreshing the remote page
  • Check your firewall settings

items.title: Where can I download the app?
items.text: See the Downloads page for all platforms.

items.title: macOS says the app is "damaged" or from an unidentified developer
items.text:
Right-click the app and select "Open", then click "Open" in the dialog. Or run:

xattr -cr /Applications/r0astr.app

items.title: Windows SmartScreen blocks the app
items.text: Click "More info" then "Run anyway".

items.title: How do I run r0astr locally?
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/piatra-automation/r0astr.git
cd r0astr
npm install
npm run dev

items.text: github.com/piatra-automation/r0astr

items.title: Ambient Textures Session
items.text:
By the r0astr team

A demonstration of layered ambient patterns using all four cards. This session shows how to build atmospheric soundscapes by combining slow pads, filtered noise, sparse melodies, and deep bass.

Patterns used:

Card 1: Slow PadCard 2: TextureCard 3: MelodyCard 4: Sub Bass 
n("0 3 7")
  .scale("C3:minor")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(slider(600, 200, 2000))
  .room(0.9)
  .slow(4)
  .gain(0.4)

s("noise")
  .lpf(slider(400, 100, 3000))
  .hpf(200)
  .room(0.7)
  .gain(0.15)

n("0 ~ 4 ~, ~ 7 ~ 3")
  .scale("C4:minor")
  .s("triangle")
  .room(0.6)
  .slow(2)
  .gain(0.35)

note("c2 ~ ~ e2")
  .s("sine")
  .lpf(200)
  .slow(2)
  .gain(0.5)

Technique: Start with just Card 1, then slowly layer in the other elements. Use the master panel filter to create sweeps across the entire mix.

items.title: Minimal Techno Jam
items.text:
By the r0astr team

Driving minimal techno demonstrating the master panel for live filter sweeps. Shows how to create tension and release using synchronized patterns.

Patterns used:

Card 1: Kick + ClapCard 2: BassCard 3: Hi-HatsCard 4: Stab 
s("bd*4, ~ cp ~ ~")
  .gain(0.85)

note("c1 ~ c1 c1")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(slider(250, 100, 800))
  .gain(0.7)

s("~ hh*16")
  .gain(slider(0.3, 0, 0.6))

n("[0,3,7] ~ ~ ~")
  .scale("C3:minor")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(1500)
  .room(0.3)
  .gain(0.4)

Technique: Drop the bass (Card 2) for breakdowns. Bring in the stab (Card 4) for tension. Use the hi-hat volume slider for dynamic changes.

items.title: Breakbeat Session
items.text:
By the r0astr team

A faster-paced demonstration showing syncopated rhythms and how multiple cards can create complex textures through simple patterns.

Patterns used:

Card 1: BreakbeatCard 2: SubCard 3: StabsCard 4: FX 
s("bd sd:1 [~ bd] sd:2, hh*8")
  .fast(1.5)
  .gain(0.8)

note("c2 ~ [c2 c2] ~")
  .s("square")
  .lpf(300)
  .gain(0.6)

n("[0,4,7] ~ [0,4,7] ~")
  .scale("C4:minor")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(slider(1000, 400, 3000))
  .gain(0.4)

s("glitch:0 ~ glitch:2 ~")
  .fast(2)
  .room(0.5)
  .gain(slider(0.3, 0, 0.5))

items.title: API Reference
items.text: r0astr provides both a REST API and a WebSocket server for real-time control and integration.

items.title: REST API
items.text:
Base URL: http://localhost:5173

All endpoints return JSON and include CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *).

items.title: Health Check
items.text:
GET /health

Response: 

{
  "status": "ok",
  "timestamp": "2025-12-14T12:00:00.000Z"
}

items.title: List Panels
items.text:
GET /api/panels

Response: 

{
  "panels": [
    {
      "id": "panel-1701234567890",
      "title": "Instrument 1",
      "code": "s(\"bd*4\")",
      "playing": true,
      "position": { "x": 0, "y": 0 },
      "size": { "w": 600, "h": 200 },
      "zIndex": 10,
      "stale": false
    }
  ]
}

items.title: Create Panel
items.text:
POST /api/panels

Body: 

{
  "title": "My Panel",
  "code": "s(\"bd*4\")",
  "position": { "x": 0, "y": 0 },
  "size": { "w": 600, "h": 200 }
}

All fields are optional. Returns { "success": true, "panelId": "panel-..." }.

items.title: Delete Panel
items.text:
DELETE /api/panels/:id

Returns { "success": true }.

Returns 403 if you attempt to delete the master panel.

items.title: Update Panel Code
items.text:
POST /api/panels/:id/code

Body: 

{
  "code": "s(\"bd*4, hh*8\")",
  "autoPlay": true
}

  • code (string, required) — New pattern code
  • autoPlay (boolean, optional) — If true, evaluate and play immediately

items.title: Control Playback
items.text:
POST /api/panels/:id/playback

Body: 

{
  "state": "play"
}

state must be "play", "pause", or "toggle".

Returns { "success": true, "currentState": "playing" }.

items.title: Connection
items.text:
ws://[host]:5173/ws

All messages are JSON. The server runs alongside the Vite dev server.

On connection, the server sends:

{
  "type": "server.hello",
  "clientId": "abc123",
  "timestamp": 1702345678000
}

items.title: Client Registration
items.text:
Register as a main interface or remote control:

{
  "type": "client.register",
  "clientType": "main"
}

clientType is "main" or "remote". Remote clients automatically receive a full_state broadcast after registration.

items.title: Commands (Remote → Server → Main)
items.text:
These messages are sent by remote clients and forwarded to the main interface:

Type Payload Description panel.play { panel: 1 } Start a panel panel.pause { panel: 1 } Pause a panel panel.toggle { panel: 1 } Toggle play/pause panel.update { panel: 1, data: { code: "..." } } Update pattern code global.stopAll — Stop all panels global.updateAll — Re-evaluate all panels master.sliderChange { sliderId, value } Change a master slider panel.sliderChange { panelId, sliderId, value } Change a panel slider

items.title: Events (Main → Server → Remote)
items.text:
These messages are broadcast from the main interface to remote clients:

Type Payload Description full_state { panels, masterCode, masterSliders } Complete state sync panel_created { panelId, title, code, position, size } New panel added panel_deleted { panelId } Panel removed panel_renamed { panelId, title } Panel title changed panel_updated { panelId, code, autoPlay } Pattern code changed playback_changed { panelId, playing } Play/pause state changed master.sliders { sliders: [...] } Master slider definitions master.sliderValue { sliderId, value } Master slider value update panel.sliders { panelId, sliders: [...] } Panel slider definitions panel.sliderValue { panelId, sliderId, value } Panel slider value update metronome.step { step: 0-15, isDownbeat } 16-step metronome tick

items.title: Error Response
items.text:
{
  "type": "error",
  "message": "Invalid JSON"
}

items.title: Security
items.text:
Local Network Only

Both the REST API and WebSocket are designed for local network use. No authentication is enforced. Do not expose to the public internet.

See Remote Control for the built-in remote interface.

items.title: Architecture Overview
items.text: Technical overview of r0astr's system design.

items.title: UI Layer
items.text:
• Panel management (create, update, delete — dynamic panel count)
  • Pattern editor with CodeMirror
  • Slider controls (auto-generated from patterns)
  • Master panel for global controls

items.title: Strudel Integration
items.text:
const { evaluate, scheduler } = repl({
  defaultOutput: webaudioOutput,
  getTime: () => ctx.currentTime,
  transpiler,
});

Key integration points:

  • repl() — Creates the pattern evaluation environment
  • evaluate() — Compiles and runs pattern code
  • scheduler — Manages pattern timing and synchronization
  • transpiler — Converts pattern code to executable JavaScript

items.title: Audio Engine
items.text:
• Single shared AudioContext (browser requirement)
  • All patterns share the same audio clock
  • Web Audio nodes for synthesis and effects

items.title: WebSocket Server
items.text:
• Runs on the Vite dev server (dev) or Electron main process (production)
  • Handles remote control connections
  • Broadcasts state changes to all clients

items.title: Key Files
items.text: File Purpose index.html Main HTML structure src/main.js Application logic, Strudel initialization src/state.js Shared state (cardStates, editorViews, AudioContext) src/managers/panelManager.js Panel lifecycle and state Map src/managers/websocketManager.js WebSocket client (browser-side) src/managers/sliderManager.js Slider rendering and synchronization src/managers/settingsManager.js localStorage persistence src/managers/skinManager.js UI skin loading and switching src/panels/panelEditor.js CodeMirror integration src/panels/panelUI.js Button state and visual indicators src/utils/eventBus.js Pub/sub event system src/websocket-server.mjs WebSocket server (Node.js side) vite.config.mjs Build config + REST API + WebSocket setup

items.title: Data Flow
items.text:
1. User edits pattern in panel
  2. Pattern sent to Strudel transpiler
  3. Transpiler generates executable code
  4. Scheduler evaluates pattern each cycle
  5. Audio output sent to Web Audio API
  6. State changes broadcast via WebSocket

items.title: Strudel Packages Used
items.text: Package Purpose @strudel/core Pattern engine @strudel/mini Mini notation parser @strudel/transpiler Code transpiler @strudel/webaudio Audio output @strudel/tonal Music theory @strudel/soundfonts SoundFont support @strudel/codemirror Editor integration

items.title: Build Pipeline
items.text:
Source Files

     ▼
Vite Development Server ──► Hot Module Replacement
     │
     ▼
Vite Build
     │
     ├──► dist/          (Electron app)
     │
     └──► dist-lite/     (Web-only, no server)
              │

items.title: r0astr Skin System
items.text: The r0astr skin system allows complete customization of the UI layout and visual theme through modular skin packages.

items.title: Architecture
items.text: Skins are self-contained packages that define: - HTML Templates - Panel layout, slider controls, UI components - CSS Theme - Visual styling, colors, spacing - Manifest - Metadata and configuration

items.title: 1. Create Skin Directory
items.text:
public/skins/
└── your-skin-name/
    ├── skin.json           # Manifest
    ├── theme.css           # Visual styling
    └── templates/
        ├── panel.html      # Panel structure
        ├── slider.html     # Slider with label
        └── slider-collapsed.html  # Compact slider

items.title: 2. Create Manifest (skin.json)
items.text:
{
  "name": "Your Skin Name",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "author": "Your Name",
  "description": "Brief description",
  "layoutType": "tree",
  "cssVariables": {
    "--primary-color": "#00ff88",
    "--panel-bg": "rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.8)"
  },
  "templates": {
    "panel": "panel.html",
    "slider": "slider.html",
    "sliderCollapsed": "slider-collapsed.html"
  },
  "hoverTargets": [
    {
      "id": "menu-trigger",
      "controls": [".top-menu-bar"],
      "hint": "subtle-glow"
    }
  ]
}

items.title: Manifest Fields
items.text:
• name - Display name for the skin
  • version - Semantic version (1.0.0)
  • author - Creator name
  • description - Brief description
  • layoutType - Layout style identifier
  • cssVariables - CSS custom properties to override
  • templates - Template file mappings
  • hoverTargets (optional) - Array of hover interaction configs

items.title: 3. Create Templates
items.text:
Templates use Mustache-style {{variable}} placeholders.

panel.html - Panel structure: 

<details{{expanded}}>
  <summary>
    <span class="panel-number-badge">{{panelNumber}}</span>
    <span class="panel-title">{{title}}</span>
    <div class="panel-actions">
      <button class="btn-playback" data-card="{{panelId}}">
        <span class="material-icons">play_arrow</span>
      </button>
      <button class="btn-delete" style="{{deleteButtonStyle}}">
        <span class="material-icons">delete</span>
      </button>
    </div>
  </summary>
  <div class="panel-editor-container">
    <div class="code-editor" id="editor-{{panelId}}"></div>
    <div class="error-message" data-card="{{panelId}}"></div>
  </div>
</details>
<div class="panel-controls-container">
  <div class="leaf-viz">
    <div id="viz-container-{{panelId}}"></div>
  </div>
</div>

slider.html - Labeled slider: 

<label>{{label}}</label>
<input type="range"
  min="{{min}}"
  max="{{max}}"
  step="{{step}}"
  value="{{value}}"
  data-slider-id="{{sliderId}}">
<span class="slider-value">{{valueFormatted}}</span>

slider-collapsed.html - Compact slider: 

<input type="range"
  min="{{min}}"
  max="{{max}}"
  step="{{step}}"
  value="{{value}}"
  data-slider-id="{{sliderId}}">

items.title: 4. Create Theme CSS (theme.css)
items.text:
Override CSS variables or add custom styles:

:root {
  --primary-color: #ff6b35;
  --panel-bg: rgba(20, 20, 30, 0.95);
  --panel-border: 2px solid var(--primary-color);
}

.level-panel {
  border-radius: 12px;
  box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.4);
}

.panel-number-badge {
  background: var(--primary-color);
  color: black;
}

items.title: 5. Configure Hover Targets (Optional)
items.text: Hover targets allow you to create interactive regions that trigger UI elements.

items.title: Hover Target Configuration
items.text:
Each hover target in the manifest has:

{
  "id": "unique-identifier",
  "controls": [".selector1", ".selector2"],
  "hint": "visual-hint-type"
}

Fields:

  • id - Unique identifier for CSS targeting
  • controls - Array of CSS selectors for elements to show on hover
  • hint - Visual hint type (see below)

items.title: Visual Hint Types
items.text:
• subtle-glow - Faint edge glow (good for edge triggers)
  • pulse - Pulsing animation (draws attention)
  • visible - Always visible (like a button)
  • banner - Uses existing element visibility
  • none - Completely invisible (discovery by accident)

items.title: CSS Implementation
items.text:
Position and style hover targets in your theme CSS:

/* Position the hover target */
.skin-hover-target[data-hover-id="menu-trigger"] {
  top: 0;
  left: 0;
  width: 20px;
  height: 100vh;
  background: linear-gradient(to right, rgba(255, 107, 53, 0.2), transparent);
  transition: all 0.3s ease;
}

/* Optional: Enhance on hover */
.skin-hover-target[data-hover-id="menu-trigger"]:hover {
  width: 30px;
  box-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(255, 107, 53, 0.5);
}

/* Position the controlled element */
.top-menu-bar {
  top: 50%;
  left: 0;
  transform: translateX(-100%) translateY(-50%);
  transition: transform 0.3s ease;
}

/* Show element on hover */
.skin-hover-target[data-hover-id="menu-trigger"]:hover ~ .top-menu-bar,
.top-menu-bar:hover {
  transform: translateX(0) translateY(-50%);
  opacity: 1;
  pointer-events: auto;
}

items.title: Example: Multi-Point Hover (Glass Skin)
items.text:
{
  "hoverTargets": [
    {
      "id": "left-edge-menu",
      "controls": [".top-menu-bar"],
      "hint": "subtle-glow"
    },
    {
      "id": "bottom-metronome",
      "controls": [".metronome-section"],
      "hint": "visible"
    }
  ]
}

CSS:

/* Left edge menu trigger */
.skin-hover-target[data-hover-id="left-edge-menu"] {
  top: 0;
  left: 0;
  width: 15px;
  height: 100vh;
  background: linear-gradient(to right, rgba(0, 212, 255, 0.15), transparent);
}

/* Bottom metronome trigger */
.skin-hover-target[data-hover-id="bottom-metronome"] {
  bottom: 0;
  left: 0;
  right: 0;
  height: 30px;
  background: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
  backdrop-filter: blur(8px);
}

items.title: Required CSS Classes
items.text:
Your templates MUST include these classes for JS functionality:

Class Purpose Required On .panel-tree Main panel container Existing in index.html .level-panel Individual panel wrapper Panel template .panel-number-badge Panel number Panel template .panel-title Editable title Panel template .code-editor CodeMirror mount point Panel template .panel-controls-container Slider/viz area Panel template .btn-playback Play/pause button Panel template .btn-delete Delete button Panel template .leaf-slider Slider wrapper Slider template .slider-value Value display Slider template

items.title: Panel Template
items.text:
• {{panelId}} - Unique panel ID (e.g., "panel-1234567890")
  • {{panelNumber}} - Panel number (0, 1, 2, ...)
  • {{title}} - Panel title (e.g., "Instrument 1")
  • {{expanded}} - " open" or "" for details state
  • {{deleteButtonStyle}} - "display: none;" for master panel, "" otherwise

items.title: Slider Template
items.text:
• {{sliderId}} - Unique slider ID
  • {{label}} - Slider label (e.g., "Cutoff")
  • {{min}} - Minimum value
  • {{max}} - Maximum value
  • {{step}} - Step size
  • {{value}} - Current value (number)
  • {{valueFormatted}} - Formatted value (e.g., "123.45")

items.title: Activating Your Skin
items.text:
1. Place skin folder in public/skins/your-skin-name/
  2. Build: npm run build
  3. Open Settings → Integrations → UI Skin
  4. Select your skin from dropdown
  5. Save settings
  6. UI hot-reloads automatically - no page refresh needed!

Development Workflow

  • CSS changes: Vite HMR updates automatically
  • Template changes: Require page reload
  • Manifest changes: Require page reload
  • Skin switching: Hot-reloads (panel state preserved)

items.title: Skin Storage
items.text:
• Browser: localStorage (r0astr-settings.skin)
  • Electron: app userData folder
  • GitHub Pages: localStorage (persists across sessions)

All modes work offline with bundled skins.

items.title: Minimal Skin
items.text: Stripped-down layout with no badges, minimal controls.

items.title: Grid Layout
items.text: Traditional card grid instead of tree structure.

items.title: WinAmp Classic
items.text: Retro WinAmp-inspired design with skinnable sprites.

items.title: Development Tips
items.text:
1. Live Reload CSS: Edit theme.css - Vite HMR updates automatically
  2. Template Changes: Require page reload
  3. Test with multiple panels: Create 3-4 panels to test overflow
  4. Check responsive behavior: Test at different window sizes
  5. Validate required classes: Missing classes break JS functionality

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text:
Skin doesn't load: - Check browser console for fetch errors - Verify skin.json is valid JSON - Check template file paths in manifest

UI broken after skin change: - Verify all required CSS classes present - Check template placeholders match expected variables - Fallback: Reset to default skin in localStorage

Hover targets not working: - Ensure z-index is above all UI elements (use z-index: 10101) - Check that hidden elements have pointer-events: none - Verify hover selectors use ~ for sibling combinators - Remember hover targets are injected as direct body children

Page won't reload: - Clear localStorage: localStorage.removeItem('r0astr-settings') - Hard refresh: Cmd+Shift+R (Mac) / Ctrl+Shift+R (Windows)

items.title: Contributing Skins
items.text: Share your skins! Create a PR with: 1. Skin folder in public/skins/ 2. Screenshot in docs/skins/ 3. Update this README with your skin description

items.title: License
items.text: Skins inherit the project's AGPL-3.0 license unless otherwise specified in skin.json.

items.title: Plugin Development
items.text: Build integrations and extensions for r0astr.

items.title: Overview
items.text: r0astr supports plugins that extend functionality through the WebSocket API. Plugins run as separate processes and communicate with r0astr over the network.

items.title: What Plugins Can Do
items.text:
• Control panels - Start, stop, and update patterns programmatically
  • Add interfaces - MIDI controllers, OSC, custom GUIs
  • Generate patterns - Algorithmic composition, sequencers
  • Integrate services - Lighting (DMX), visuals, external apps
  • Monitor state - React to pattern changes and playback events

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Node.js 18+
  • Familiarity with r0astr API
  • Understanding of WebSocket communication

items.title: Plugin Structure
items.text:
my-r0astr-plugin/
├── package.json        # Dependencies and metadata
├── src/
│   └── index.js        # Entry point
├── README.md           # Documentation
└── LICENSE             # License file

items.title: Minimal package.json
items.text:
{
  "name": "my-r0astr-plugin",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "My r0astr plugin",
  "main": "src/index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node src/index.js"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "ws": "^8.0.0"
  }
}

items.title: Hello World Plugin
items.text:
A complete working plugin:

// src/index.js
const WebSocket = require('ws');

const R0ASTR_URL = process.env.R0ASTR_URL || 'ws://localhost:5173/ws';

class R0astrPlugin {
  constructor() {
    this.ws = null;
    this.connected = false;
  }

  connect() {
    console.log(`Connecting to r0astr at ${R0ASTR_URL}...`);
    this.ws = new WebSocket(R0ASTR_URL);

    this.ws.on('open', () => {
      console.log('Connected to r0astr!');
      this.connected = true;
      this.onConnected();
    });

    this.ws.on('message', (data) => {
      const message = JSON.parse(data);
      this.onMessage(message);
    });

    this.ws.on('close', () => {
      console.log('Disconnected from r0astr');
      this.connected = false;
      // Attempt reconnect after 5 seconds
      setTimeout(() => this.connect(), 5000);
    });

    this.ws.on('error', (err) => {
      console.error('Connection error:', err.message);
    });
  }

  onConnected() {
    // Request current state on connect
    this.send({ type: 'state:get' });
  }

  onMessage(message) {
    console.log('Received:', message.type);

    switch (message.type) {
      case 'state:current':
        console.log('Current state:', message.payload);
        break;
      case 'event:panel-state':
        console.log('Panel changed:', message.payload);
        break;
    }
  }

  send(message) {
    if (this.connected) {
      this.ws.send(JSON.stringify(message));
    }
  }

  // Panel control methods
  startPanel(panelId) {
    this.send({
      type: 'panel:start',
      payload: { panelId }
    });
  }

  stopPanel(panelId) {
    this.send({
      type: 'panel:stop',
      payload: { panelId }
    });
  }

  updatePattern(panelId, pattern) {
    this.send({
      type: 'panel:update',
      payload: { panelId, pattern }
    });
  }

  stopAll() {
    this.send({ type: 'playback:stop-all' });
  }
}

// Start the plugin
const plugin = new R0astrPlugin();
plugin.connect();

// Example: Start panel 1 after 3 seconds
setTimeout(() => {
  console.log('Starting panel 1...');
  plugin.startPanel('panel-1');
}, 3000);

items.title: Running the Plugin
items.text:
npm install
npm start

items.title: Lifecycle Events
items.text: The r0astr server broadcasts events your plugin can listen to:

items.title: Panel State Changed
items.text:
Fired when any panel starts or stops:

{
  "type": "event:panel-state",
  "payload": {
    "panelId": "panel-1",
    "playing": true
  }
}

items.title: Pattern Updated
items.text:
Fired when a panel's pattern changes:

{
  "type": "event:pattern-update",
  "payload": {
    "panelId": "panel-1",
    "pattern": "s(\"bd*4\")"
  }
}

items.title: Subscribing to Events
items.text:
Events are automatically broadcast to all connected clients. Simply handle them in your onMessage handler:

onMessage(message) {
  switch (message.type) {
    case 'event:panel-state':
      this.handlePanelState(message.payload);
      break;
    case 'event:pattern-update':
      this.handlePatternUpdate(message.payload);
      break;
  }
}

items.title: Available Commands
items.text: Command Payload Description panel:start { panelId } Start a panel panel:stop { panelId } Stop a panel panel:update { panelId, pattern } Update pattern playback:stop-all - Stop all panels playback:tempo { bpm } Set tempo state:get - Request current state

items.title: Response Types
items.text:
Type Payload When state:current Full state object After state:get event:panel-state Panel status When panel starts/stops error Error details On invalid command

See API Reference for complete documentation.

items.text:

    cd r0astr
npm run dev

npm start

items.title: Debugging Tips
items.text:

    R0ASTR_URL=ws://192.168.1.100:5173/ws npm start

items.title: Testing Checklist
items.text:

  • [ ] Panel commands work (start, stop, update)

items.title: MIDI Controller
items.text:
Map MIDI CC to sliders, notes to panel triggers:

const midi = require('midi');

// ... MIDI setup ...

midiInput.on('message', (deltaTime, message) => {
  const [status, cc, value] = message;

  if (status === 0xB0) { // CC message
    // Map CC to slider value
    const sliderValue = value / 127;
    // Update pattern with new value
  }

  if (status === 0x90) { // Note on
    const panelId = `panel-${(cc % 4) + 1}`;
    plugin.startPanel(panelId);
  }
});

items.title: OSC Bridge
items.text:
Connect to SuperCollider, Max/MSP, TouchOSC:

const osc = require('osc');

const oscPort = new osc.UDPPort({
  localPort: 57121
});

oscPort.on('message', (oscMessage) => {
  if (oscMessage.address === '/r0astr/panel/1/start') {
    plugin.startPanel('panel-1');
  }
});

items.title: Pattern Sequencer
items.text:
Programmatically change patterns over time:

const patterns = [
  's("bd*4")',
  's("bd*4, hh*8")',
  's("bd*4, hh*8, ~ sd ~ sd")'
];

let index = 0;

setInterval(() => {
  plugin.updatePattern('panel-1', patterns[index]);
  index = (index + 1) % patterns.length;
}, 4000); // Change every 4 seconds

items.title: Visualizer
items.text:
React to pattern changes for visuals:

onMessage(message) {
  if (message.type === 'event:panel-state') {
    // Update visuals based on which panels are playing
    updateVisualization(message.payload);
  }
}

items.title: Skins
items.text: r0astr features a skin system that allows customization of the UI layout and visual theme through modular skin packages.

items.title: What Are Skins?
items.text:
Skins are self-contained packages that define:

  • HTML Templates — Panel layout, slider controls, UI components
  • CSS Theme — Visual styling, colors, spacing, animations
  • Manifest — Metadata, configuration, and CSS variable overrides

Unlike traditional themes that only change colors, r0astr skins can completely restructure the UI layout while maintaining compatibility with the underlying JavaScript.

items.title: Default (Tree Layout)
items.text:
The standard r0astr interface with collapsible tree-structured panels, inline sliders, and floating visualizers.

  • Vertical tree layout with drag-to-reorder
  • Collapsible details panels
  • Badge-based panel numbering
  • Top banner hover trigger for menu access

items.title: Glass (Glassmorphism)
items.text:
Modern frosted glass design with translucent panels and backdrop blur.

  • Dark purple gradient background
  • Frosted glass panels with backdrop blur
  • Glowing cyan accents
  • Left-edge menu trigger (vertical slide-out)
  • Bottom metronome trigger (glass strip)
  • Pulsing glow animations on playing panels

items.title: Switching Skins
items.text:
1. Click CONFIG in the top menu (hover the top banner area to reveal it)
  2. Navigate to Integrations
  3. Select a skin from the UI Skin / Theme dropdown
  4. Click Save

Skins hot-reload automatically — no page refresh needed. Panel state is preserved during the transition.

items.title: Loading Sequence
items.text:
1. App loads settings from localStorage
  2. Fetch manifest from /skins/{skinName}/skin.json
  3. Inject skin's theme.css into document head
  4. Parse HTML templates into rendering functions
  5. Templates cached in memory for fast rendering

items.title: Storage
items.text:
• Browser: localStorage as part of r0astr-settings
  • Electron: userData/r0astr-settings.json

All modes work offline with bundled skins.

items.title: Skin Structure
items.text:
A skin is a folder in public/skins/ containing:

your-skin-name/
├── skin.json           # Manifest (name, version, templates, cssVariables, hoverTargets)
├── theme.css           # Visual styling
└── templates/
    ├── panel.html      # Panel structure
    ├── slider.html     # Labeled slider
    └── slider-collapsed.html  # Compact slider

Templates use Mustache-style {{variable}} placeholders. For JavaScript functionality to work, templates must include specific CSS classes:

Class Purpose .panel-tree Main container (in index.html) .level-panel Panel wrapper .panel-title Editable title .code-editor CodeMirror mount point .btn-playback Play/pause button .panel-controls-container Sliders area

items.title: Technical Details
items.text:
• SkinManager: src/managers/skinManager.js
  • Template Compilation: Simple regex-based Mustache engine
  • Integration: panelManager and sliderManager use skinManager.render()
  • Initialization: main.js loads skin before rendering UI

items.title: License
items.text: Skins inherit the project's AGPL-3.0 license unless otherwise specified in skin.json.

items.title: Troubleshooting
items.text: Common issues and solutions for r0astr.

items.title: No Sound Playing
items.text:
1. Check browser permissions - Click somewhere on the page first. Browsers require user interaction before playing audio.
  2. Check volume - Ensure your system volume and the pattern's .gain() value are set appropriately.
  3. Check the console - Open browser DevTools (F12) and look for errors.

items.title: Audio is Choppy or Glitchy
items.text:
• Close other tabs - Audio processing is CPU-intensive
  • Use Chrome or Firefox - These have the best Web Audio support
  • Reduce pattern complexity - Simpler patterns use less CPU

items.title: Samples Not Loading
items.text:
• Sample loading happens on first use - you may see console messages
  • Wait a moment and try playing again
  • Check your internet connection

items.title: Pattern Not Updating
items.text:
1. Make sure you clicked Play after editing
  2. Check for syntax errors in the console
  3. Verify your pattern ends with a valid function call

items.title: Syntax Errors
items.text:
Common mistakes:

// Wrong - missing quotes
s(bd sd)

// Correct
s("bd sd")

// Wrong - missing dot
s("bd sd")gain(0.5)

// Correct
s("bd sd").gain(0.5)

items.title: Can't Connect from iPad/Phone
items.text:
1. Same network - Both devices must be on the same WiFi
  2. Use network IP - Use http://192.168.x.x:5173/remote.html, not localhost
  3. Check firewall - Your computer's firewall may block connections

items.title: Remote Shows "Disconnected"
items.text:
• The main app must be running
  • Try refreshing the remote page
  • Check that WebSocket port is not blocked

items.title: macOS: "App is damaged" or "unidentified developer"
items.text:
Right-click the app and select "Open", then click "Open" in the dialog.

Or run in Terminal: 

xattr -cr /Applications/r0astr.app

items.title: Windows: SmartScreen Warning
items.text: Click "More info" then "Run anyway".

items.title: Linux: AppImage Won't Run
items.text:
Make it executable: 

chmod +x r0astr-*.AppImage
./r0astr-*.AppImage

items.text: Browser Support Chrome Full Firefox Full Safari Full (macOS 14+) Edge Full Mobile Chrome Basic Mobile Safari Basic

items.title: Still Stuck?
items.text:

  • See the FAQ
  • Open a new issue with details about your problem

items.title: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants
items.text:
r0astr is a multi-panel live coding interface built on top of these projects:

  • Tidal Cycles by Alex McLean — the pattern language that started it all
  • Strudel by Felix Roos — Tidal Cycles ported to JavaScript, the engine under r0astr's hood

For pattern syntax, mini notation, samples, scales, and effects, go straight to the source:

Strudel Learn Pattern Reference Tidal Cycles Docs

The pages below cover what r0astr adds on top of Strudel: panels, synchronization, the master panel, remote control, and multi-instrument workflows.

  • Concepts

    Understand the mental model: panels, patterns, and synchronization.

    Read Concepts

  • Multi-Instrument

    Use multiple panels together for layered compositions.

    Multi-Instrument Guide

  • Master Panel

    Control global tempo and effects across all panels.

    Master Panel Guide

  • Remote Control

    Control r0astr from a tablet, phone, or any device on your network.

    Remote Control

  • Example Arrangements

    Complete multi-panel tracks you can copy and paste.

    Example Arrangements

  • Troubleshooting

    Common issues and solutions.

    Troubleshooting

items.title: Learning Path
items.text:
1. Start with Concepts — Understand how r0astr organizes instruments
  2. Learn Strudel patterns — Work through the Strudel workshop
  3. Explore Multi-Instrument — Combine panels for full arrangements
  4. Use the Master Panel — Control global parameters
  5. Try an arrangement — Copy a full track from Example Arrangements

items.title: Concepts
items.text: Understand the mental model behind r0astr to make the interface intuitive.

items.title: The Big Picture
items.text:
r0astr is a multi-instrument live coding environment. Think of it as having multiple independent instruments on stage, all connected to the same metronome. You write code to control each instrument, and they all play together in perfect sync. You can create as many panels as you need.

graph TB
 subgraph r0astr["r0astr Interface"]
 MP[" Master Panel<br/>TEMPO, Global Effects"]
 C1[" Panel 1<br/>Drums"]
 C2[" Panel 2<br/>Bass"]
 C3[" Panel 3<br/>Melody"]
 CN[" Panel N<br/>..."]
 end
 CLOCK((" Shared Clock"))
 MP --> CLOCK
 CLOCK --> C1
 CLOCK --> C2
 CLOCK --> C3
 CLOCK --> CN

items.title: Cards
items.text: Each panel is an independent instrument in r0astr.

items.title: What is a Panel?
items.text:
A panel is a self-contained unit that includes:

  • A code editor where you write patterns
  • A Play/Pause button to control playback
  • Slider controls for real-time parameter adjustments

items.title: Independence
items.text:
Panels operate independently:

  • Different patterns: Each panel can play completely different music
  • Separate controls: Start, stop, and modify each panel without affecting others
  • Own parameters: Each panel can have its own sliders and settings

items.title: Typical Setup
items.text:
A common starting point:

Panel Common Use Panel 1 Drums and percussion Panel 2 Bass line Panel 3 Melody or lead Panel 4 Ambient or effects

This is just a suggestion — use panels however you like, and add more as needed.

Learn more in the Multi-Instrument Guide

items.title: Synchronization
items.text: All panels share a single audio clock. This is the key to how r0astr works.

items.title: Shared Clock
items.text:
• Every panel receives timing from the same master clock
  • When you start a new panel, it automatically syncs with already-playing patterns
  • No drift or timing issues between instruments

items.title: What This Means in Practice
items.text:
1. Start Panel 1 with a drum pattern
  2. Start Panel 2 with a bass line — it immediately locks to the beat
  3. Start Panel 3 with a melody — also perfectly in sync
  4. All three play together as if they were designed as one piece

items.title: Quantization
items.text:
When you press Play on a panel:

  • The pattern doesn't start immediately at a random point
  • It waits for the next cycle boundary
  • This ensures musical timing is always correct

items.title: Master Panel
items.text: The master panel sits above all panels and provides global controls.

items.title: Purpose
items.text:
The master panel affects everything at once:

  • TEMPO: Set the speed (in CPS - cycles per second)
  • Global Sliders: Create variables like SLIDER_LPF that all panels can use
  • Master Effects: Apply filters or effects to the entire mix

items.title: TEMPO Control
items.text:
The master panel is where you set the global tempo using a slider:

// In Master Panel
let TEMPO = slider(30, 15, 45); // 30 CPS, range 15-45

All panels automatically follow this tempo.

items.title: Global Sliders
items.text:
Define sliders in the master panel that any panel can reference:

// Master Panel
let SLIDER_LPF = slider(800, 100, 5000);

// Any panel can use it:
s("bd*4").lpf(SLIDER_LPF)

This lets you control parameters across all instruments with a single slider.

Learn more in the Master Panel Guide

items.title: Pattern Lifecycle
items.text: Understanding how patterns work as you edit and play them.

items.title: Step by Step
items.text:
1. Write: Type or paste a pattern into a panel's editor
  2. Play: Click the Play button to start the pattern
  3. Listen: Audio plays immediately through your speakers
  4. Edit (optional): Modify the pattern while it's playing
  5. Update: Changes take effect on the next cycle
  6. Pause: Click Pause to stop only that panel

items.title: Live Coding
items.text:
The magic of r0astr is live coding:

  • Edit patterns while they play
  • Hear changes almost instantly
  • No need to stop and restart
  • Experiment in real-time

Pro Tip

Make small changes and listen. This is the essence of live coding - evolving your music incrementally as you play.

items.title: How They Connect
items.text:
Let's tie all the concepts together:

  1. You write patterns in each panel using Strudel's mini notation
  2. Panels are independent — each runs its own pattern
  3. The master panel provides global controls like TEMPO
  4. A shared clock keeps everything synchronized
  5. Live updates let you evolve your music in real-time

items.title: Next Steps
items.text:
• Pattern Syntax

Learn the mini notation language for writing patterns.

Strudel Learn

  • Multi-Instrument

Advanced techniques for using multiple cards together.

Multi-Instrument Guide

  • Master Panel

Deep dive into global controls and sliders.

Master Panel Guide

items.title: Example Arrangements
items.text: Multi-panel compositions that show how r0astr's panels work together. Copy each pattern into the corresponding panel.

items.title: Minimal Setup
items.text:
A simple, cohesive arrangement.

Panel 1 (Drums): 

s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd, hh*8").gain(0.8)

Panel 2 (Bass): 

note("c2 ~ c2 e2").s("sawtooth").lpf(300).gain(0.6)

Panel 3 (Lead): 

n("0 2 3 5 3 2").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").gain(0.5)

Panel 4 (Pad): 

n("0 3 7").scale("C3:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(600).room(0.8).slow(4).gain(0.4)

items.title: Dark Techno
items.text:
Heavy, driving techno arrangement.

Panel 1 (Drums): 

s("bd*4, ~ cp ~ ~, hh*16").gain(0.85)

Panel 2 (Bass): 

note("c1 ~ c1 c1").s("sawtooth").lpf(slider(250, 100, 800)).gain(0.7)

Panel 3 (Stab): 

n("[0,3,7] ~ ~ ~").scale("C3:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(1500).room(0.3).gain(0.4)

Panel 4 (Texture): 

s("noise").lpf(slider(300, 50, 2000)).gain(0.1)

items.title: Ambient Chill
items.text:
Relaxed, atmospheric arrangement.

Panel 1 (Light Percussion): 

s("~ hh ~ hh, ~ ~ rim ~").gain(0.4)

Panel 2 (Bass): 

note("c2 ~ ~ e2").s("sine").lpf(400).gain(0.5).slow(2)

Panel 3 (Melody): 

n("0 ~ 4 ~, ~ 7 ~ 5").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").room(0.7).gain(0.4).slow(2)

Panel 4 (Pad): 

n("0 3 7 10").scale("C3:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(500).room(0.95).slow(8).gain(0.3)

items.title: Building a Multi-Panel Composition
items.text:
• Stay in key: Use the same scale (e.g., C:minor) across all panels
  • Frequency separation: Keep bass low, melody in the mid range, pads spread wide
  • Dynamics: Use .gain() sliders on each panel for real-time mix control
  • Start sparse: Begin with one or two panels, then bring others in gradually

items.title: Experimenting
items.text:
• Change .fast(2) to .fast(4) for double speed
  • Swap scales: C:minor → C:major
  • Add effects: .room(0.5) or .delay(0.25)
  • Add sliders: .lpf(slider(800, 100, 4000))
  • Use the Master Panel for global tempo and effects

items.title: Master Panel Guide
items.text: Learn to control global tempo and create shared parameters across all panels.

items.title: What is the Master Panel?
items.text:
The master panel sits above all panels and provides controls that affect everything at once:

  • TEMPO: Controls the speed of all patterns
  • Global Sliders: Variables that any panel can reference
  • Stop All: Stops all panels simultaneously

Think of the master panel as the conductor - it sets the tempo and overall tone, while each panel (musician) plays their own part.

items.title: What TEMPO Does
items.text: TEMPO controls how fast patterns play across all panels. When you adjust TEMPO, every playing pattern speeds up or slows down together.

items.title: Default Setup
items.text:
The master panel includes a TEMPO slider:

let TEMPO = slider(30, 15, 45);
Parameter Value Meaning Default 30 Starting speed Min 15 Slowest setting Max 45 Fastest setting

items.title: How Speed Works
items.text:
r0astr uses CPS (Cycles Per Second) internally. You don't need to understand the math - just know that:

  • Higher TEMPO = faster patterns
  • Lower TEMPO = slower patterns
  • All cards follow the same tempo

items.title: Practical Use
items.text:
1. Start with the default tempo
  2. Use the slider to find a comfortable speed
  3. All patterns automatically adjust

Performance Tip

Subtle tempo changes during a performance can add dynamics. Try slowly increasing tempo to build energy.

items.title: Global Effect Sliders
items.text: Create sliders in the master panel that control parameters across all panels.

items.title: Why Use Global Sliders?
items.text:
Instead of adding separate filter sliders to each panel, create one in the master panel:

  • Single control affects everything
  • Consistent effect across all instruments
  • Great for sweeps and buildups

items.title: Creating a Global Slider
items.text:
In the master panel code:

let SLIDER_LPF = slider(800, 100, 5000);

This creates: - A slider named SLIDER_LPF - Starting at 800 Hz - Range from 100 Hz to 5000 Hz

items.title: Using in Panels
items.text:
Reference the variable name in any panel:

// Panel 1
s("bd*4").lpf(SLIDER_LPF)

// Panel 2
note("c2 ~ c2 e2").s("sawtooth").lpf(SLIDER_LPF)

// Panel 3
n("0 2 4").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").lpf(SLIDER_LPF)

Now one slider controls the filter on all three panels.

items.title: Multiple Global Sliders
items.text:
Add as many as you need:

// Master Panel
let SLIDER_LPF = slider(800, 100, 5000);
let REVERB = slider(0.3, 0, 1);
let MASTER_GAIN = slider(0.8, 0, 1);

Use them in panels:

note("c2").lpf(SLIDER_LPF).room(REVERB).gain(MASTER_GAIN)

items.title: Step 1: Define in Master Panel
items.text:
Write your slider definitions in the master panel:

let FILTER = slider(1000, 200, 4000);
let SPACE = slider(0.5, 0, 1);

items.title: Step 2: Use in Panel Patterns
items.text:
Reference the variable names (exactly as written) in any panel:

// This panel uses both global sliders
note("c2 e2 g2")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(FILTER)
  .room(SPACE)
  .gain(0.6)

items.title: Step 3: Control in Real-Time
items.text: Adjust the sliders while patterns play. All cards using those variables update instantly.

items.title: Naming Conventions
items.text:
Use clear, descriptive names:

Good Why SLIDER_LPF Clear it's a slider, controls low-pass MASTER_GAIN Indicates global volume REVERB Simple and obvious TEMPO Special - controls global speed

items.title: Basic Syntax
items.text:
let NAME = slider(default, min, max);
Part Description let NAME Variable name to use in panels slider() Creates a slider control default Starting value min Minimum value max Maximum value

items.title: Examples
items.text:
Filter Control (Hz): 

let FILTER_CUTOFF = slider(800, 100, 8000);

Volume Control (0-1): 

let VOLUME = slider(0.7, 0, 1);

Reverb Amount (0-1): 

let ROOM_SIZE = slider(0.4, 0, 1);

Speed Modifier: 

let SPEED = slider(1, 0.5, 4);

items.title: Complete Example
items.text:
Master Panel: 

let TEMPO = slider(30, 15, 45);
let LPF = slider(1200, 100, 4000);
let REVERB = slider(0.3, 0, 0.9);
let MASTER = slider(0.8, 0, 1);

Panel using globals: 

note("c2 e2 g2 e2")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(LPF)
  .room(REVERB)
  .gain(MASTER)

items.title: What It Does
items.text: The Stop All button immediately stops every playing pattern across all panels.

items.title: When to Use
items.text:
• End of performance: Clean stop
  • Reset: Start fresh
  • Emergency: Something sounds wrong

items.title: Behavior
items.text:
When you click Stop All:

  1. All cards stop immediately
  2. Panels remain paused (not reset)
  3. Code in each panel is preserved
  4. Click Play on individual cards to restart

items.title: Advanced: How Master Panel Works
items.text:
For Advanced Users

This section explains the technical implementation. You don't need to understand this to use r0astr effectively.

items.title: Parsing Differences
items.text:
The master panel works differently from cards:

Feature Panels Master Panel Code evaluation Strudel transpiler Regex parsing Pattern execution Yes No Slider detection Automatic Manual parsing

items.title: Why Regex?
items.text: The Strudel transpiler can block when used in the master panel context. To avoid this, r0astr parses master panel code with regex to extract slider definitions.

items.title: TEMPO Special Handling
items.text:
TEMPO isn't just a variable - it controls the scheduler:

  1. Master panel code defines: let TEMPO = slider(...);
  2. r0astr detects the TEMPO variable
  3. Changes are converted to CPS (cycles per second)
  4. The scheduler's speed is updated: scheduler.setCps(value)

items.title: Slider Detection Pattern
items.text:
Master panel sliders must follow this exact format:

let VARIABLE_NAME = slider(default, min, max);

The parser looks for: - let keyword - Variable name (letters, numbers, underscores) - = slider( - Three numeric values

For detailed technical information, see the Architecture documentation.

items.title: Global Variables and Functions
items.text: Beyond sliders, the master panel can define variables, constants, and functions that are accessible in all panels.

items.title: Defining Global Variables
items.text:
Use let, const, or var to define values available everywhere:

// Master Panel
let SCALE = "E:minor:pentatonic"
const ROOT = "c3"
let NOTES = [0, 2, 4, 5, 7]

Use them in any panel:

// Panel 1
n("0 2 4").scale(SCALE)

// Panel 2
note(ROOT).s("bass")

items.title: Defining Global Functions
items.text:
Create functions in the master panel for complex orchestration:

// Master Panel
function WHICH_PHASE() {
  return Math.floor(scheduler.now() / 4) % 4;
}

function activeWhen(phase) {
  return ref(() => WHICH_PHASE() === phase ? 1 : 0);
}

Use in panels:

// This pattern fades in only during phase 0
n("0 2 4").scale(SCALE).gain(activeWhen(0))

items.title: Example: Phase-Based Orchestration
items.text:
Master Panel: 

let SCALE = "E:minor:pentatonic"

function WHICH_PHASE() {
  return Math.floor(scheduler.now() / 4) % 4;
}

function activeWhen(phase) {
  return ref(() => WHICH_PHASE() === phase ? 1 : 0);
}

Panels can then create patterns that fade in/out based on the current phase:

stack(
  n("0 2 4").scale(SCALE).s("piano").gain(activeWhen(0)),
  n("0 3 5").scale(SCALE).s("strings").gain(activeWhen(1)),
  s("bd sd bd sd").gain(activeWhen(2)),
  n("0 7 12").scale(SCALE).s("pad").gain(activeWhen(3))
)

items.title: Master Panel Syntax
items.text:
// Required for tempo control
let TEMPO = slider(30, 15, 45);

// Optional global sliders
let SLIDER_LPF = slider(800, 100, 5000);
let REVERB = slider(0.3, 0, 1);
let GAIN = slider(0.7, 0, 1);

items.title: Panel Usage
items.text:
// Reference global sliders by name
note("c2").lpf(SLIDER_LPF).room(REVERB).gain(GAIN)

items.title: Multi-Instrument Guide
items.text: Learn to use multiple cards together for layered compositions.

items.title: Panel Setup
items.text:
r0astr gives you independent panels — create as many as you need. Think of each panel as a musician in a band: each plays their own part, but they all follow the same beat.

Panel Typical Role Panel 1 Drums / Percussion Panel 2 Bass Panel 3 Melody / Lead Panel 4 Ambient / Texture

This is a common starting point, but you can use panels however you like.

items.title: Individual Control
items.text:
Each panel has its own Play/Pause button:

  • Play: Starts the pattern in that panel
  • Pause: Stops only that panel (others keep playing)

items.title: What Happens to Other Panels?
items.text: Nothing. When you start or stop a panel, all others continue unchanged. This is the key to live performance — you can bring instruments in and out dynamically.

items.title: Stop All
items.text: The master panel has a Stop All button that stops every panel at once.

items.title: Synchronization
items.text: All panels share a single audio clock. This is fundamental to how r0astr works.

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
1. The master panel sets the global tempo
  2. Every panel receives timing from this shared clock
  3. When you press Play on a panel, it waits for the next cycle boundary
  4. The pattern joins in perfect sync with anything already playing

items.title: Why This Matters
items.text:
• No drift: Panels never slowly go out of sync
  • Musical timing: New patterns start at musically sensible moments
  • Confidence: You can add/remove instruments knowing they'll stay in time

items.title: Example Scenario
items.text:
1. Press Play on Panel 1 (drums) — it starts immediately
  2. Press Play on Panel 2 (bass) — it waits for the next cycle, then joins in sync
  3. Both patterns now play together, locked to the same beat
  4. Press Pause on Panel 1 — drums stop, bass continues
  5. Press Play on Panel 1 — drums rejoin on the next cycle

items.title: Complementary Pattern Design
items.text: Design patterns that work together, not against each other.

items.title: Role of Drums (Panel 1)
items.text:
Drums provide the rhythmic foundation:

  • Establish the groove
  • Define the pulse
  • Use samples: bd, sd, hh, cp
// Simple four-on-floor
s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd, hh*8").gain(0.8)

items.title: Role of Bass (Panel 2)
items.text:
Bass provides the harmonic foundation:

  • Lock with the kick drum
  • Establish the key/root
  • Use low frequencies (lpf keeps it warm)
// Sub bass matching the drums
note("c2 ~ c2 e2").s("sawtooth").lpf(300).gain(0.6)

items.title: Role of Melody (Panel 3)
items.text:
Melody provides interest and movement:

  • Sit in the mid-to-high frequency range
  • Use scales to stay in key
  • Add motion and variation
// Minor scale melody
n("0 2 3 5 3 2").scale("C4:minor").s("triangle").gain(0.5)

items.title: Role of Ambient (Panel 4)
items.text:
Ambient provides texture and space:

  • Fill in the background
  • Use reverb and slow patterns
  • Keep volume low to not overwhelm
// Slow evolving pad
n("0 3 7").scale("C3:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(600).room(0.9).slow(4).gain(0.4)

items.title: Samples vs Synths
items.text: Type Best For Example Samples (s()) Drums, percussion, realistic sounds s("bd sd hh") Synths (note()) Bass, melody, pads note("c3 e3")

items.title: Frequency Separation
items.text:
Keep your mix clear by separating frequencies:

Role Frequency Range How Bass Low (60-300 Hz) Use .lpf(300) Drums Wide Samples are pre-EQ'd Melody Mid (300-3000 Hz) Use higher octaves (C4, C5) Ambient Variable Use reverb (.room())

items.title: Matching Sounds to Roles
items.text:
• Bass: sawtooth or square with low-pass filter
  • Melody: triangle or sawtooth with mid-range filter
  • Pads: sawtooth with heavy reverb and slow patterns

items.title: Cross-Panel Pattern References
items.text: Panels can reference patterns from other panels by their title. This enables powerful orchestration where one panel controls multiple patterns.

items.title: How It Works
items.text:
1. Name your panels: Double-click the panel title to rename (e.g., "BASS", "LEAD")
  2. Write patterns: Each panel's pattern is automatically registered under its title
  3. Reference in other panels: Use the title name to access the pattern

items.title: Example Setup
items.text:
Panel titled "BASS": 

n("0 2 0 5").scale("E:minor").s("sawtooth").lpf(400)

Panel titled "LEAD": 

n("0 4 7 11").scale("E:minor").s("triangle")

Panel titled "COMBO": 

stack(BASS, LEAD.fast(2))

The COMBO panel now plays both patterns stacked together, with LEAD playing twice as fast.

items.title: Evaluation Order
items.text:
Panels evaluate top to bottom based on their position in the panel list. This means:

  • A panel can only reference patterns from panels above it
  • Drag panels to reorder if needed
  • The master panel always evaluates first

items.title: Dynamic Updates
items.text:
When you update a panel's pattern:

  1. The pattern re-registers under its title
  2. Any playing panels below that reference it automatically re-evaluate
  3. Changes cascade through your arrangement

items.title: Pattern Manipulation
items.text:
Once referenced, you can transform patterns:

// In a panel titled "MIX"
stack(
  BASS.gain(0.7),
  LEAD.fast(2),
  DRUMS.slow(2)
)

items.title: Reactive Cross-References
items.text:
Use ref() for values that update continuously:

stack(
  BASS.gain(ref(() => WHICH_PHASE() === 0 ? 1 : 0)),
  LEAD.gain(ref(() => WHICH_PHASE() === 1 ? 1 : 0))
)

This creates patterns that fade in/out based on a function defined in the master panel.

items.title: Building Up
items.text:
A classic performance technique:

  1. Start with drums - Establish the groove
  2. Add bass - Lock in the harmonic foundation
  3. Layer melody - Add interest
  4. Finish with ambient - Fill out the sound

items.title: Creating Dynamics
items.text:
Use stops for contrast:

  • Drop the bass: Pause Panel 2 for 8 bars, then bring it back
  • Drums only: Stop everything except drums for a breakdown
  • Ambient interlude: Stop drums and bass, let ambient breathe

items.title: Live Variation
items.text:
While patterns are playing:

  • Edit the pattern code for instant changes
  • Adjust sliders for real-time parameter tweaks
  • Swap out patterns entirely for different sections

items.title: Full Arrangement Example
items.text: Here's a complete four-panel arrangement. Copy each pattern into its respective panel.

items.title: Panel 1: Drums
items.text:
s("bd*4, ~ sd ~ sd, hh*8")
  .gain(0.8)

Establishes the beat with kick, snare, and hi-hats.

items.title: Panel 2: Bass
items.text:
note("c2 ~ c2 e2")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(slider(300, 100, 800))
  .gain(0.6)

Sub bass locked to the kick, with a slider for filter sweeps.

items.title: Panel 3: Melody
items.text:
n("0 2 3 5 3 2")
  .scale("C4:minor")
  .s("triangle")
  .lpf(1200)
  .gain(slider(0.5, 0, 1))

Minor scale melody with volume control.

items.title: Panel 4: Ambient
items.text:
n("0 3 7")
  .scale("C3:minor")
  .s("sawtooth")
  .lpf(600)
  .room(0.9)
  .slow(4)
  .gain(0.4)

Slow chord pad with heavy reverb for atmosphere.

items.title: How They Work Together
items.text:
• Key: All patterns use C minor
  • Tempo: All locked to the master panel tempo
  • Frequency: Bass low, melody mid, ambient spread
  • Dynamics: Sliders allow real-time adjustment

items.title: Performance Walkthrough
items.text:
Try this sequence:

  1. Start drums (Panel 1) - Let it groove for 4 bars
  2. Add bass (Panel 2) - Foundation locked in
  3. Add melody (Panel 3) - Music takes shape
  4. Add ambient (Panel 4) - Full arrangement
  5. Drop melody - Pause Panel 3 for tension
  6. Bring melody back - Release
  7. Stop all except ambient - Breakdown
  8. Restart drums - Build back up
